does: This paper demonstrates that prompt injection vulnerability in tool-augmented LLM agents is fundamentally dependent on the interaction between model and injection surface, not the surface alone. The authors hold attack payloads byte-identical and deliver them through two distinct surfaces—tool outputs and tool descriptions—across 13 LLMs from six families and four task suites. They find dramatic variation in vulnerability across models for the same injection channel, such that GPT-4.1 shows 96% vulnerability on tool outputs but only 4% on tool descriptions, while Gemini-3-Flash exhibits the inverse pattern at 20% and 98% respectively. A variance decomposition of 6,830 attack attempts reveals that the injection surface alone explains zero percent of outcome variation, while model-surface interactions account for 16.7% of variation, establishing that vulnerability is fundamentally a property of the pairing between model and surface rather than an intrinsic property of either alone.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on adversarial robustness and prompt injection attacks against language models. Prior research identified prompt injection as a critical vulnerability class in tool-augmented agents, typically evaluating attack success rates on single surfaces like tool outputs without systematic comparison across injection channels. The paper builds on earlier work characterizing LLM vulnerabilities to instruction-following attacks and responds to the limitation that current evaluations report monolithic vulnerability scores by surface, treating that metric as model-inherent. It connects to broader research on contextual factors affecting LLM behavior and the interaction between model architecture and adversarial stimulus presentation, while directly challenging the assumption that vulnerability assessment requires testing only one injection surface per model.
enables: This findings enable more rigorous and nuanced vulnerability assessments for tool-augmented LLM agents by revealing that single-surface evaluations can grossly mischaracterize true security properties. Future work can develop model-surface-aware defensive strategies tailored to specific vulnerability patterns rather than generic mitigations. The variance decomposition framework provides a foundation for systematically studying which architectural or training choices drive differential vulnerability across surfaces, potentially enabling researchers to predict vulnerable configurations without exhaustive testing. This work also opens directions for understanding why certain models systematically fail on particular surfaces, which could inform both architectural improvements and better evaluation methodologies. Practitioners deploying LLM agents can use these insights to prioritize which surfaces to harden given their specific model choice.
